content.text: Moving an agentic AI system from a successful proof-of-concept into production is one of the highest-risk transitions in enterprise delivery. A PoC validates a hypothesis — often under relaxed constraints — while production must meet security, reliability, and operational standards the PoC was never designed for. A structured exit-criteria checklist turns that transition from a judgment call into a governed gate, giving stakeholders a shared language for what "production-ready" actually means.
Why It Matters

Without explicit exit criteria, teams routinely promote PoCs that pass the demo but break under real usage. The failure modes are predictable: a PoC built on a single developer's laptop with a hard-coded API key reaches production still holding that secret; a PoC that worked at 5 concurrent users starts timing out at 50; a model that performed well on curated demo inputs produces harmful outputs on the edge cases only production traffic surfaces. Every one of these failures erodes stakeholder trust in agentic AI more broadly, not just in the individual project.

A formal checklist addresses this by requiring evidence, not attestation. The team does not claim that security has been reviewed — they provide the pentest report. They do not assert that the system handles load — they provide load-test results with percentile latency numbers. Evidence-based gates are harder to wave through and easier to audit after an incident.
Benefits
Shared definition of done.
Engineering, product, security, and compliance teams agree on criteria before the PoC starts, eliminating last-minute disputes about what "ready" means.
Incident prevention.
Gate items directly map to real incident categories. Checking rollback before launch means rollback has been tested, not just documented.
Audit trail.
A completed checklist with evidence links and sign-off timestamps provides a defensible record for compliance reviews and post-incident analysis.
Remediation clarity.
FAIL items become concrete engineering tasks. A failing security gate produces a Jira ticket with an owner, not a vague "security needs attention" comment.
Reusability.
The same checklist structure applies to every subsequent agentic project, accumulating institutional knowledge about which criteria catch real problems.
Trade-offs

The main risk of a formal checklist is bureaucratic cargo cult: teams learn to satisfy the form rather than the intent. A security gate that is checked by asking "did you do a security review?" and accepting "yes" without evidence provides the appearance of governance without the substance. Checklist items must be operationalized as evidence requirements, not attestations.

A second risk is that overly rigid checklists slow down low-risk projects. A small internal automation agent used by 10 people does not need the same gate depth as a customer-facing AI that handles medical triage. The solution is a tiered checklist: a base tier for all agentic systems and additional tiers activated by risk factors such as external user exposure, sensitive data, or autonomous action.
How It Works

A PoC exit checklist is structured around gate dimensions, each covering a category of production risk. Each item in a dimension has three possible states: PASS (criterion fully met with evidence), PARTIAL (workaround exists and risk is accepted with a documented remediation date), or FAIL (blocker that must be resolved before promotion). The overall gate passes when all FAIL items are resolved and the number of PARTIAL items is within an agreed threshold — typically no more than two, none in the security dimension.

The six standard dimensions for agentic AI systems are: security, scale, observability, rollback, data pipeline, and stakeholder sign-off. Each dimension is reviewed by the appropriate domain owner: security by the AppSec team, scale by the platform or SRE team, data pipeline by the data engineering team, and stakeholder sign-off by the product and business owners. The gate review is typically a 90-minute meeting with a pre-distributed evidence pack.

When items fail, the output is a remediation sprint — a time-boxed effort to address blockers — followed by a re-review of only the failing dimensions. This avoids re-running the full gate for every iteration, reducing review overhead while maintaining rigor.
Architecture

attrs.alt: PoC exit criteria gate flow: PoC Complete feeds into Exit Criteria Review across 6 dimensions (Security, Scale, Observability, Rollback, Data Pipeline, Stakeholders). PASS routes to Production Readiness then Production; FAIL routes to Remediation Sprint then back to review.

content.text: The diagram shows the complete exit-criteria flow. A completed PoC enters the review gate where six dimensions are evaluated in parallel. Items that score FAIL route into a remediation sprint before returning for re-assessment. Items that score PASS or PARTIAL (within threshold) advance to a production readiness sign-off step, after which the system enters a monitored production rollout. The scoring legend at the bottom defines the minimum threshold: all FAIL criteria resolved, at most two PARTIAL items, and the security gate always PASS with no exceptions.
When to Use It
Any agentic system that will operate in production against real user traffic or business data requires a formal exit gate, regardless of scope.
Apply a lightweight version (base tier only) for internal-only tools with no sensitive data and limited blast radius.
Apply the full checklist for customer-facing agents, systems handling PII or financial data, and systems that take autonomous action with real-world consequences.
Run a subset of checklist items on major feature additions post-launch — scale and security gates should re-run whenever the system's capability or exposure surface changes materially.
How to Implement
Define checklist dimensions and items before the PoC begins, not after. Teams that define criteria post-PoC are unconsciously anchoring on what was already achieved.

For each item, specify the evidence required. "Security review complete" is an attestation; "AppSec pentest report attached, all critical and high findings closed or risk-accepted" is an evidence requirement.
Assign dimension owners before the PoC starts so reviewers know they have a gate review responsibility and can flag concerns early.
Store the checklist in a tracked document (Confluence, Notion, or a Git-committed YAML) so the evidence links, owners, and sign-off dates are durable.
Run a pre-gate dry run two weeks before the scheduled gate meeting. Surface blockers early enough to address them without delaying the launch date.
Examples
Checklist as YAML

# poc-exit-checklist.yaml
# Store in repo root; update status and evidence links before gate meeting.

project: customer-support-agent
poc_completed: 2025-06-15
gate_meeting: 2025-06-29
overall_status: PENDING  # PASS | PARTIAL | FAIL

dimensions:
  security:
    owner: appsec-team@company.com
    items:
      - id: sec-01
        criterion: "AppSec pentest completed; all critical/high findings closed or risk-accepted"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "https://wiki/pentest-report-2025-06"
      - id: sec-02
        criterion: "No API keys or model credentials in source code or container images"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "gitleaks scan clean — CI artifact #4821"
      - id: sec-03
        criterion: "Prompt injection attack surface documented and mitigations in place"
        status: PARTIAL
        evidence: "mitigation PR merged; fuzzing suite added to CI"
        remediation_owner: backend-lead
        remediation_date: 2025-07-10
      - id: sec-04
        criterion: "Data retention and PII handling reviewed by legal/privacy"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "Privacy review sign-off email — thread 2025-06-12"

  scale:
    owner: platform-team@company.com
    items:
      - id: scale-01
        criterion: "Load test at 2x expected peak; p99 latency within SLO"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "k6 load-test report: p99=1.8s, SLO=3.0s"
      - id: scale-02
        criterion: "Autoscaling policy configured and tested"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "HPA config in infra/k8s/hpa.yaml, smoke tested in staging"
      - id: scale-03
        criterion: "Token cost projection at peak load within budget"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "Cost model spreadsheet linked in project wiki"

  observability:
    owner: sre-team@company.com
    items:
      - id: obs-01
        criterion: "Request latency, error rate, and token cost dashboards live in Grafana"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "https://grafana/d/agent-prod-dashboard"
      - id: obs-02
        criterion: "PagerDuty alerts configured for error rate > 1% and p99 > SLO"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "Alert rules in monitoring/alerts.yaml"
      - id: obs-03
        criterion: "Structured logging enabled; trace IDs propagated end-to-end"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "logging PR #312 merged"

  rollback:
    owner: engineering-lead@company.com
    items:
      - id: rb-01
        criterion: "Rollback procedure documented in runbook"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "runbook/rollback.md in repo"
      - id: rb-02
        criterion: "Rollback tested in staging within 30 days"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "Staging rollback drill 2025-06-10, completed in 4 min"
      - id: rb-03
        criterion: "Feature flag or canary mechanism in place for gradual rollout"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "LaunchDarkly flag 'agent-v1-enabled' configured"

  data_pipeline:
    owner: data-team@company.com
    items:
      - id: dp-01
        criterion: "Data pipeline SLA met for 30 consecutive days in staging"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "Pipeline monitoring: 99.7% uptime last 30 days"
      - id: dp-02
        criterion: "Data drift detection enabled on model inputs"
        status: PARTIAL
        evidence: "Evidently AI integration in progress"
        remediation_owner: data-lead
        remediation_date: 2025-07-05

  stakeholders:
    items:
      - id: sh-01
        criterion: "Product owner sign-off obtained"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "Sign-off email from product-owner 2025-06-28"
      - id: sh-02
        criterion: "Legal and compliance review complete"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "Legal review memo 2025-06-20"
      - id: sh-03
        criterion: "Engineering lead and SRE lead signed off"
        status: PASS
        evidence: "Gate meeting minutes 2025-06-29"
Python Gate Evaluator

# evaluate_gate.py — read poc-exit-checklist.yaml, print gate summary
# pip install pyyaml

import sys
import yaml
from pathlib import Path
from collections import defaultdict


def evaluate_checklist(path: str) -> None:
    data = yaml.safe_load(Path(path).read_text())
    project = data["project"]
    dimension_results: dict[str, dict] = {}

    overall_pass = True
    total_fail = 0
    total_partial = 0

    print(f"\n=== Exit Criteria Gate: {project} ===")

    for dim_name, dim in data["dimensions"].items():
        items = dim.get("items", [])
        fail_items = [i for i in items if i["status"] == "FAIL"]
        partial_items = [i for i in items if i["status"] == "PARTIAL"]
        pass_items = [i for i in items if i["status"] == "PASS"]

        dim_status = "PASS"
        if fail_items:
            dim_status = "FAIL"
            overall_pass = False
        elif partial_items:
            dim_status = "PARTIAL"

        total_fail += len(fail_items)
        total_partial += len(partial_items)

        icon = {"PASS": "✓", "PARTIAL": "~", "FAIL": "✗"}[dim_status]
        print(f"  [{icon}] {dim_name.upper():<20} PASS={len(pass_items)} PARTIAL={len(partial_items)} FAIL={len(fail_items)}")

        for item in fail_items:
            print(f"      FAIL: {item['id']} — {item['criterion']}")
        for item in partial_items:
            owner = item.get("remediation_owner", "TBD")
            date = item.get("remediation_date", "TBD")
            print(f"      PARTIAL: {item['id']} — owner={owner} due={date}")

    print(f"\n  Totals: FAIL={total_fail}  PARTIAL={total_partial}")

    # Security gate: no partials allowed
    sec_partials = [
        i for i in data["dimensions"].get("security", {}).get("items", [])
        if i["status"] in ("PARTIAL", "FAIL")
    ]
    if sec_partials:
        overall_pass = False
        print("  BLOCKED: security dimension has unresolved items (zero tolerance)")
    if total_partial > 2:
        overall_pass = False
        print("  BLOCKED: more than 2 PARTIAL items across all dimensions")

    verdict = "GATE PASSED — APPROVED FOR PRODUCTION" if overall_pass else "GATE FAILED — REMEDIATION REQUIRED"
    print(f"\n  >>> {verdict}\n")
    sys.exit(0 if overall_pass else 1)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    evaluate_checklist(sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "poc-exit-checklist.yaml")
Real-World Example

A financial services firm built a PoC for an agent that triages customer service tickets and drafts preliminary responses. The PoC ran for six weeks, processed 500 synthetic tickets, and showed 40% reduction in analyst time-to-first-response. The gate review ran three weeks before the planned production date.

The security gate failed on two items: the PoC had hard-coded model API keys in the application configuration, and prompt injection mitigations had not been tested against adversarial customer inputs. These became tracked remediation tasks with two-week deadlines. The data pipeline gate scored PARTIAL because the production data feed had only been running for 15 days rather than the required 30. The team accepted this as a monitored risk with a commitment to halt rollout if pipeline reliability dropped below 99% in the first month.

Two weeks later, the security items were resolved and the gate re-ran on the security dimension only. With all blockers cleared and one PARTIAL item (data pipeline) within threshold, the gate passed and production launch proceeded with a 5% canary rollout. The structured gate had added two weeks to the schedule but prevented a credential exposure that would have been a regulatory incident.
Common Mistakes
Defining Criteria After the PoC

Teams that write the checklist after seeing PoC results unconsciously include only criteria the PoC already meets. The checklist becomes a rubber stamp. Define criteria before the PoC begins, ideally as part of the project charter.
Accepting PARTIAL on Security Items

Security gate items should have a zero-PARTIAL policy. "We know the prompt injection vector exists but it's unlikely to be exploited" is how production incidents happen. Apply risk acceptance only with written documentation from the security team — never informally in a gate meeting.
Skipping the Rollback Test

A rollback procedure that has never been executed is not a rollback procedure — it is an untested theory. The gate should require that rollback was executed end-to-end in a staging environment and that the elapsed time was recorded. Untested rollbacks routinely fail in the first real incident.
No Evidence Links

A checklist with statuses but no evidence links is an attestation document, not a gate. Each PASS item must link to something reviewable: a test report, a scan result, a pull request, a signed-off email. This makes the gate auditable and honest.
Best Practices
Version the checklist template in Git alongside the project, so changes to the criteria are tracked and reviewable.
Run a pre-gate dry run two weeks before the scheduled review. This surfaces blockers early enough to fix without delaying launch.
Re-run only the failing dimensions after remediation. Re-running the full gate for each iteration wastes reviewer time and trains teams to treat it as a formality.
Include a tier modifier: add a column for "risk tier" (internal / customer-facing / regulated) that activates additional items automatically, keeping low-risk projects lightweight.
Automate what you can. The Python evaluator above can run in CI against the YAML file, blocking merges to a production branch until the gate passes.
Retrospect the checklist after the first three productions launches. Items that never fail are likely too easy; items that frequently produce trivial remediations may be over-specified.
Performance & Scalability

The checklist process itself has low overhead when run as a structured YAML document with automated evaluation. The dominant cost is the human time required to generate evidence: running load tests, executing rollback drills, and obtaining stakeholder sign-offs. A well-run program should budget two to three weeks of elapsed time between PoC completion and gate meeting, with the evidence collection running in parallel to other preparation work.

For organizations running multiple agentic projects simultaneously, a shared evidence registry — a wiki page or database where recurring evidence artifacts (platform security certifications, shared pipeline SLA records) are stored once and referenced by multiple checklists — significantly reduces per-project overhead.
Tooling & Ecosystem
YAML + Git.
Storing the checklist as YAML in the project repository keeps it versioned, diff-able, and CI-evaluable without requiring dedicated tooling.
Jira / Linear.
FAIL items map directly to tickets. The evaluator script can output Jira-formatted issue descriptions, reducing the friction of turning gate failures into tracked work.
k6 / Locust.
Load testing tools for generating the scale gate evidence. k6 produces JSON reports that can be referenced as evidence links.
gitleaks / truffleHog.
Secret scanning tools for the security gate. Both can be run in CI and produce scan reports usable as evidence.
LaunchDarkly / Flagsmith.
Feature flag systems that enable the canary rollout mechanism required by the rollback gate.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Rigor
Speed
Auditability
Best for
Structured exit checklist (YAML)
High
2–3 weeks elapsed
Full — Git history + evidence links

Informal team review
Low
Hours
Minimal — meeting notes only
Low-risk internal tools
Third-party readiness audit
Very high
4–8 weeks
Maximum — external report
High-stakes systems (healthcare, finance)

Medium
Minutes (automated)
High for technical items
Complement to structured checklist, not replacement
Security & Reliability

The security gate dimension deserves special treatment because agentic systems introduce security risks that traditional software reviews may not cover. Prompt injection — where a malicious user crafts input that hijacks the agent's behavior — is not covered by standard AppSec checklists. The gate should explicitly require testing against adversarial inputs, not just standard OWASP categories.
Require secret scanning (gitleaks, truffleHog) as a CI gate, not just a one-time check. Secrets have a way of re-appearing after refactoring.
Test the rollback procedure in staging against a realistic failure scenario — not just "delete the deployment" but the full sequence including alert firing, on-call response, and traffic rerouting.

Document the blast radius of every tool the agent can invoke. An agent that can write to a production database or send emails to customers has a high blast radius and requires stricter rollback and permission gate criteria.
Mental Model

Think of the exit checklist as an aircraft pre-flight inspection, not a bureaucratic hurdle. A pre-flight inspection does not slow down safe aircraft — it catches the rare but catastrophic condition where something looks fine but is not. The PoC is the maiden flight in simulator conditions. The exit gate is the systematic inspection before taking passengers.

The six gate dimensions map to the six categories of things that kill agentic AI in production: a security breach destroys trust; a scale failure destroys availability; missing observability makes incidents invisible; an untested rollback makes incidents unrecoverable; a flaky data pipeline makes quality unpredictable; and missing stakeholder sign-off makes the launch a political liability. The checklist is evidence that each of those six risks has been examined, not assumed away.
Key Takeaways
Define exit criteria before the PoC begins, not after — post-hoc criteria are unconsciously anchored to what the PoC already achieved.
Each criterion requires evidence, not attestation — a scan report, test result, or signed approval, not a verbal confirmation.
Security gate items carry zero tolerance for PARTIAL status; all other dimensions allow at most two PARTIAL items with documented owners and dates.
FAIL items become tracked engineering tasks; re-review only the failing dimensions after remediation to keep overhead proportionate.
Store the checklist as YAML in the project repository so it is versioned, diff-able, and evaluable by a CI script.
Apply a tiered model: base criteria for all systems, additional items triggered by risk factors such as external user exposure, PII, or autonomous action.
Retrospect the checklist after the first three production launches — items that never fail are too easy; items producing trivial remediations may be over-specified.
